pages.text: Use of power plant bottom ash as ﬁne and coarse aggregates in high-strength concrete H.K. Kim, H.K. Lee * Department of Civil and Environmental Engineering, Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology, Guseong-dong, Yuseong-gu, Daejeon 305-70 1, Republic of Korea article info Article history: Received 3 February 2010 Received in revised form 26 May 2010 Accepted 19 June 2010 Keywords: High-strength concrete Bottom ash By-product Mechanical properties Modulus of elasticity Flexural strength abstract In this study, experiments have been carried out to evaluate the utilization of bottom ash (by-product of power plant) as ﬁne and coarse aggregates in high-strength concrete with compressive strength of 60–80 MPa. Firstly, the chemical and physical characteristics of bottom ash particles, such as chemical compositions, speciﬁc gravity and SEM images, were investigated. Further experiments were conducted by replacing ﬁne and coarse bottom ash with normal sand and gravel varying in percentages (25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%). The effect of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash on the ﬂow characteristics and density of con- crete mixture was investigated in the aspect of particle shapes and paste absorption of bottom ash. Mechanical properties, such as compressive strengths and modulus of elasticity and ﬂexural strength of high-strength concrete with bottom ash were evaluated. It was found that the slump ﬂow of fresh concrete was slightly decreased from 530 mm to 420 mm when coarse bottom ash was replaced 100% of normal coarse aggregates, while ﬁne bottom ash did not affect the slump ﬂow. Moreover, it also showed that both of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash aggregates had more inﬂuence on the ﬂexural strength than compressive strength. /C2112010 Elsevier Ltd. All rights reserved. 1. Introduction Lightweight concrete with high-strength has many advantages [1–4]. If lightweight concrete with high-strength is realized, the section size of members may be reduced due to lighter dead load of upper structures [4]. This may permit not only large space avail- ability but also a reduction in cement and reinforcement quantity [1,5]. Therefore, high-strength, lightweight concrete can allow the construction of mega structures such as hyper long span bridges and skyscrapers [1]. Lighter and smaller precast structural ele- ments would also allow easier handling and reduce transportation costs, and lightweight concrete shows high thermal, ﬁre, and noise insulation [1]. Furthermore, offshore structures used for oil pro- duction require buoyant lightweight concrete members that can be towed easily [6]. In general, the density of high-strength concrete is higher than that of normal strength concrete, being around 100–250 kg/m 3, due to larger unit cement content, lower air content, and compact microstructure. Reducing the density of high-strength concrete, however, presents signiﬁcant technical challenges. Accordingly, high-strength, lightweight concrete has been studied extensively for many years. Kaar et al. studied the stress–strain relationship and ﬂexural stress distribution for ultimate strength design for normal and lightweight concrete with high-strength ranging from 24.5 MPa to 86.1 MPa [7]. Holms reported a long term strength gain of high-strength concrete with expended shale [8]. In a study by Bürge, lightweight concrete with condensed silica fume was introduced [9]. Slate et al. reported on high-strength lightweight concrete with a compressive strength of 55 MPa (8000

porated silica fume [13]. Ahmad and his colleagues presented the ﬂexural behavior of a high-strength lightweight concrete beam with a compressive strength of 37–80 MPa and a density of 2000 kg/m3 [14,15]. In this study, artiﬁcial expended slate was used as coarse aggregates. In other notable works Kılıç et al. presented a structural high- strength lightweight concrete made with basaltic-pumice (scoria) aggregates and Demirdag and Gunduz produced high performance masonry units with concrete containing ﬁne and coarse volcanic slag aggregates [16,17]. Kou et al. investigated the fresh and hard- ened properties of concrete prepared with recycled plastic waste from scrapped PVC pipe [18]. Meanwhile, there have been few studies on the use of fuel ash, a by-product of coal-ﬁred power plants, as an aggregate for high- strength, lightweight concrete. Fuel ash, such as ﬂy ash and bottom ash, has many proven advantages as an aggregate and admixture in concrete, and is utilized in concrete blocks and road constructions [19,20]. Roughly eighty-thousand tons of bottom ash, which ac- counts for 10.4% of by-products of coal-ﬁred power plants, is ap- plied as a lightweight aggregate in European countries [20–23]. Moreover, Kayali recently reported an investigation of concrete with a lightweight aggregate manufactured by sintering ﬂy ash [5]. The concrete specimens showed good mechanical properties, including 28 day compressive strength of 44–62 MPa correspond- ing with a density of 1720–1800 kg/m 3 and 3–85 mm slump [5]. In a previous study by our group a lightweight cellular concrete with high-strength of up to 50 MPa was manufactured using bot- tom ash as ﬁne and coarse aggregates [24]. In this study, experiments have been carried out to evaluate the utilization of bottom ash (by-product of power plant) as ﬁne and coarse aggregates in high-strength concrete with compressive strength of 60–80 MPa. Firstly, the chemical and physical charac- teristics of bottom ash particles, such as chemical compositions, speciﬁc gravity and SEM images, were investigated. Further exper- iments were conducted by replacing ﬁne and coarse bottom ash with normal sand and gravel varying in percentages (25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%). The effect of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash on the ﬂow characteristics and density of concrete mixture was investi- gated in the aspect of particle shapes and paste absorption of bot- tom ash. Mechanical properties, such as compressive strengths and modulus of elasticity and ﬂexural strength of high-strength, light- weight concrete with bottom ash were evaluated. The high-strength, lightweight concrete in this study was spe- ciﬁcally designed for marine structure applications with high dura- bility. All concrete cast in this study have higher binder content than other high-strength concrete with the compressive strength of 60–80 MPa. Moreover, silica fume was 15% of the total binder materials, whereas most codes specify a maximum of 10% for silica fume used in concrete except in special applications such as for shotcrete or for suppressing alkali-silica reaction. The reason for this is to densify the microstructures for enhancing water, gas and ion penetration resistances. The additional problems of this mixture design, such as high shrinkage and early temperature ris- ing, would be controlled in the future research. 2. Experimental program 2.1. Materials Type I Portland cement (C), blast furnace slag (BFS), and silica fume (SP) were used in different pro

Fig. 4 , there existed fewer small pores (below 100 nm) than large pores. It was ob- served that the nanostructure of bottom ash was quite dense, because nano-scale pores and defects were rare in bottom ash particles. 2.2. Mixture proportions Concrete mixtures were designed as shown in Table 3 and tested for their fresh and hardened characteristics. The specimens were designed to evaluate the effects of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash aggregates on high-strength lightweight concrete. Crushed sand and crushed gravel were replaced with four different volume frac- tions (25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%) of FBA and CBA, respectively. Details of the mix pro- portions are given in Table 2 and are denoted with speciﬁc codes. Labels ‘F’ and ‘S’ represent ﬁne and coarse aggregates, respectively. The numbers ‘0’, ‘25’, ‘50’, ‘75’, and ‘100’ refer to the replaced volume percentages. Dosages of water, silica fume, and superplastisizer were ﬁxed as 187 kg/m 3, 143 kg/m 3, and 14 kg/m 3 for all spec- imens, respectively, constituting weight proportions of 30.8%, 20%, and 2.5% of ce- ment in the control specimen. 2.3. Experimental details All batches were mixed in a 60 L capacity mixer. Fine and coarse aggregates were dried in an oven for more than 1 day prior to the experiment in order to pre- vent outﬂow of water absorbed in the aggregates, which would affect the W/C ratio of the cement binders. The materials, excluding water and superplastisizer, were mixed for 3 min. The water and superplastisizer were then added and mixed for 5 min more. The slump ﬂow of the fresh concrete was tested following the ASTM C 1611 speciﬁcation. The density of concrete was balanced to obtain the relative density ( q/q0). In the present study, the relative density q/q0 is deﬁned as the ratio of the density of specimens ( q) to the theoretical density ( q0). The theoretical density ( q0) is the sum of the unit weight of materials in the concrete, as listed in Table 3 . The theo- retical density is not only related to the air content in the cement paste but also the packing condition of the aggregates and binder powder particles. 100 mm /C2 200 mm cylinder-type and 100 mm /C2 100 mm /C2 500 mm rectangu- lar-type specimens were cast for measuring compressive and ﬂexural strength, respectively. Fresh concrete was covered with a plastic sheet and cured for 3 days in the laboratory and then demolded. The specimens were cured in a water tank at 20 ± 1 /C176C. The compressive strength of the specimens was measured at 7 and 28 days of curing, following the ASTM C39 test methods. The static modulus of elasticity E was calculated based on the stress corresponding to 40% of the ulti- mate load, according to ASTM C469-02. The modulus of rupture R was measured after 28 days of curing, using a simple beam with center-point loading following ASTM C293-08. The width, depth, and pure span length of the beam specimens were determined to be 100 mm, 100 mm, and 400 mm, respectively. 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Effects of bottom ash on ﬂow characteristics of fresh concrete Fig. 5 presents the ﬂow characteristics of fresh concrete with different volumes of FBA and CBA aggregates. Cement paste with the same mix proportion was applied for all batches, and thus the experiment could be designed such that the ﬂow characteris- tics were only affected by replacement of FBA and CBA. As shown in Fig. 5 , the slump ﬂow values of fresh concrete was decreased by up to 100 mm when 100% of CBA was used. The decrease

m3 when both 100% FBA and 100% CBA were applied. The decrease in density was larger in the case of concrete with CBA than con- crete with FBA. The density of concrete decreased to 109 kg/m 3 (4.6%) and 228 kg/m 3 (9.6%) when ﬁne aggregate was replaced with 100% FBA and coarse aggregates were replaced with 100% CBA, respectively. This was due to the volume proportion of coarse aggregate being greater than that of ﬁne aggregate, despite though more cement paste was absorbed on the surface of CBA and the pores in CBA were ﬁlled by cement paste with the result that air bubbles were forced out. The decrease in density of concrete containing both FBA and CBA was equivalent to the sum of the decreases of density of con- crete with only FBA and only CBA. The decrease in density of spec- imen ‘F100-C100’ ( /C0 383 kg/m3) was similar to the sum of the decreases in density of specimens ‘F100-C0’ ( /C0 109 kg/m3) and ‘F0-C100’ ( /C0 228 kg/m3). As shown in Fig. 10 , the relative density was almost constant at 0.98 even though the percentage of bottom ash was changed. The relative density of concrete, in general, can be decreased due to the generation of pores in the cement matrix (i.e., entrained air and entrapped air). This means that the amount of air bubbles in the cement matrix, which come out from FBA and CBA, as shown in Fig. 7 a, can be negligible in this study. 3.3. Effects of bottom ash on compressive strength and modulus of elasticity of concrete Fig. 11 shows the effects of FBA and CBA on the compressive strength at 7 days and 28 days. The 7-day and 28-day compressive strengths were around 60 MPa and 70 MPa, respectively, and were not strongly affected by the replacement of FBA and CBA. This was also observed when FBA was used together with CBA (specimens ‘F50-C50’ and ‘F100-C100’). Many experimental studies have shown conﬂicting results regarding the effect of bottom ash aggre- gate on compressive strength [5,27,28]. Yüksel et al. reported that the 28-day compressive strength was decreased from 52 MPa to Fig. 6. Surface shapes of fresh concrete with (a) and without (b) bottom ash aggregates. Fig. 7. Cross section of hardened concrete with CBA (a) and crushed gravel (b) (height in 180 lm, width in 240 lm). Effects of bottom ash on density of concrete. H.K. Kim, H.K. Lee / Construction and Building Materials 25 (2011) 1115–1122 1119

imen. Also, the modulus of elasticity of concrete with 100% CBA was 31.8 GPa, or 77.5% that of the control specimen. In the case of concrete with 100% FBA and 100% CBA, the modulus of elasticity was 20.1 GPa, which was 49.0% that of the control specimen. A large range of decrease in the modulus of elasticity with the appli- cation of a lightweight aggregate was reported by Andrade et al. [29]. Andrade et al. found that the modulus of elasticity decreased from 25.8 GPa to 8.9 GPa when the replacement ratio of bottom ash aggregate was increased to 100% and the unit weight of cement was ﬁxed at 300 kg/m 3 [29]. From these results, it is evident that FBA and CBA strongly affect the elastic behavior of concrete. 3.4. Effects of bottom ash on ﬂexural strength of concrete Fig. 13 shows the effect of FBA and CBA aggregates on the ﬂex- ural strength of high-strength concrete. Even though the compres- sive strength was not changed, the ﬂexural strength of concrete almost linearly decreased as the replacement ratio of FBA and CBA was increased. The modulus of rupture was decreased to 19.5% and 24.0% in accordance with 100% replacement of normal aggregates with FBA and CBA, respectively. Also, the modulus of rupture of concrete was deceased to 12.8% with both 100% FBA and 100% CBA (specimen ‘F100-C100’) replacement, but this was not as signiﬁcant as the decrease of modulus of elasticity, as noted in Section 3.3. Rossignolo et al. similarly reported that the ﬂexural strengths of high-strength concrete were decreased even though the compressive strengths were stable in accordance with the lightweight aggregate used [30]. Fig. 13 shows the fracture sections of high-strength concrete with normal and bottom ash aggregates. The fracture section of concrete with bottom ash aggregates shows a ﬂatter surface than concrete with normal aggregates. This means that the cracks easily propagated though bottom ash particles, as shown in Fig. 14 b, while the normal aggregates were hard to penetrate, and conse- quently the direction of crack propagation was changed by the existence of normal aggregates. Therefore, the ﬂexural strength of concrete was decreased when bottom ash aggregates were ap- plied. However, advanced studies have to be carried out to investi- gate the mechanisms of crack nucleation and propagation during compressive and ﬂexural loading for concrete incorporating bot- tom ash. 4. Concluding remarks The present paper had presented results of the experimental study to evaluate the feasibility of utilizing bottom ash as ﬁne and coarse aggregates in high-strength concrete with compressive strength in a range of 60–80 MPa. Firstly, the chemical and physical characteristics of bottom ash particles, such as chemical compositions, speciﬁc gravity and SEM images, were investigated. Further experiments were conducted by replacing ﬁne and coarse bottom ash with normal sand and gravel varying in percentages (25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%). The effect of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash on the ﬂow characteristics and density of concrete mixture was investigated in the aspect of particle shapes and paste absorption of bottom ash. Mechanical properties, such as compressive strengths and modulus of elasticity and ﬂexural strength of high-strength, lightweight concrete with bottom ash were evaluated. The following conclusions have been drawn. (1) The ﬂow characteristics of fresh concrete were slightly reduced by the use of coarse bottom ash, whereas the effect of ﬁne bottom as

tion effect between aggregates decreased due to the cement paste being absorbed into the bottom ash particles. On the other hand, the slump ﬂow values were unchanged as the replacement ratio of ﬁne bottom ash was increased. Fine bottom ash particles absorbed a smaller amount of cement paste and water due to its lower porosity and water absorp- tion. Furthermore, ﬁne bottom ash absorbed only a very small amount of cement paste and water during mixing. (2) The densities of hardened concrete linearly decreased as the replacement ratio of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash was increased. Also, the decrease in density of concrete contain- ing both ﬁne and coarse bottom ash was equivalent to the sum of the decreased the density of concrete with only ﬁne bottom ash and only coarse bottom ash. The density of high- strength concrete in this study was less than 2000 kg/m 3 when both 100% ﬁne bottom ash and 100% coarse bottom ash were applied. (3) The 7-day and 28-day compressive strengths were around 60 MPa and 70 MPa, respectively, and were not strongly affected by the replacement of FBA and CBA. In this study, the proportion of cement paste was higher than that in other preceding works so that the effects of bottom ash aggregates were smaller than in the previous studies. Whereas, it was found that the modulus of elasticity decreased in accordance with an increase of replacement of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash aggregates. In the case of concrete with 100% ﬁne bot- tom ash and 100% coarse bottom ash, the modulus of elastic- ity was decreased down to 49.0% that of the control specimen. (4) Even though the compressive strength was unchanged, the ﬂexural strength of concrete almost linearly decreased as the replacement ratio of the ﬁne and coarse bottom ash was increased. The modulus of rupture was decreased to 19.5% and 24.0% in accordance with 100% replacement of normal aggregates with FBA and CBA, respectively. The cracks caused by ﬂexural load easily propagated though bot- tom ash particles while the normal aggregates were hard to penetrate, and consequently the direction of the crack prop- agation was changed by the existence of normal aggregates. 40 MPa with use of 50% bottom ash as a ﬁne aggregate [27]. Kura- ma and Kaya reported that the compressive strength of concrete was decreased from 41 MPa to 29 MPa in accordance with the replacement of bottom ash as ﬁne aggregate up to 50% [28]. On the contrary, Kayali reported an increase of compressive strength and indirect tensile strength of concrete with ﬂy ash aggregates (FAA, corresponding with bottom ash in this study) [5]. The compressive strength of concrete was increased from 58 MPa to 67 MPa when granite natural aggregates were replaced up to 100% with FAA ﬁne and coarse aggregates, even though the W/C ratio, dosage of silica fume, and superplasticizer were ﬁxed (Kayali, 2008). In this study, the proportion of cement paste was higher than that in other preceding works. Approximately 522 L/ m 3 of cement paste (water, binder materials, and superplastisizer) was applied in this study, whereas the amount ranged from 294 L/ m3 to 368 L/m 3 in Kurama and Kaya and Kayali [5,28]. Therefore, the effects of bottom ash aggregates were smaller than in the pre- vious studies. To clarify the effect of bottom ash aggregates on the compressive strength of concrete, further research is needed with respect to aggregate proportions, water absorption condition of the aggregate, and the S/a ratio, important factors in lightweight aggregate concrete. Fig. 12 presents the modulus of elasticity correlated with FBA and CBA for high-strength concrete. It was found that the modulus of elasticity decreased in accordance with an increase of replace- ment of ﬁne and coarse bottom ash aggregates. The modulus of elasticity of concrete without FBA and CBA was around 41.1 GPa. The modulus of elasticity decreased to 34.9 GPa when FBA was re- placed at 100%, and this value was 84.9% that of the control spec- 0.900 0.920 0.940 0.960 0.980 1.000 0.0 20.0 40.0 60.0 80.0 100.0 Relative density Replacement of bottom ash (vol. %) FBA replacement CBA replacement FBA + CBA replacement Fig. 10. shape and rougher surface than normal aggregate particles, as shown in Fig. 2 . In Lee and Kim, it was reported that the friction between rougher particles was higher than that in the case of using normal aggregate, and this higher friction force hindered the ﬂow characteristics of fresh concrete. Second, the amount of both water and cement paste for lubrication between aggregates was decreased because some of the water and cement paste would be drawn into the bottom ash particles. Fig. 6 presents the surface shapes of fresh concrete with and without bottom ash. Black spots appeared on the surface of the fresh concrete with bottom ash, due to suction of the water and cement paste by bottom ash, as shown in Fig. 6 a. Moreover, as shown in the images taken by the optical microscope with reﬂected light in Fig. 7 , water and cement paste was entrapped in craters on the CBA particles during the mixing and curing, whereas there were no craters to absorb the cement paste on normal aggregates. Due to the pores and craters absorbed in the fresh cement paste and the water during mixing process, some amount of air bub- bles, which escaped from pores of craters, was entrapped in ce- ment paste as shown in Fig. 8 . Cement paste used in this study had a high viscosity because of lower W/C and higher binder con- tent than normal concrete as shown in mix proportion ( Table 3 ). Air bubbles thus became harder to escape from the cement paste. In addition, water absorption on the surface of the bottom ash might decrease the ﬂow characteristics. Before the setting of the fresh concrete, a water ﬁlm thinner than 20 lm formed on the interface area between the cement paste and aggregates (Interfa- cial transition zone, ITZ), because of lower water absorption of the surface of the aggregates [26]. The W/C ratio on the ITZ was in- creased due to the water ﬁlm and a white rim around the aggre- gates appeared in the case of concrete with normal aggregates, as shown in Fig. 7 b. In contrast, water absorption of bottom ash was 5–8%, which was much higher than that of normal aggregates, as shown in Table 2. Water in cement paste can be absorbed into pores on the surface of bottom ash because there is some amount of nano size pores smaller than cement of silica fume particles, as presented in Figs. 3 and 4 . Therefore, the white rim of concrete with bottom ash was thinner than that in the case of concrete with normal aggre- gate as shown in Fig. 7a. Thus, the effective W/C ratio of the cement paste with bottom ash aggregates was lower than that of cement paste with normal aggregates. Also, the Slump ﬂow of fresh con- crete with bottom ash aggregates was lower than in the case of concrete with normal aggregate as shown in Fig. 5 . While replacement of CBA affected the ﬂow characteristics of concrete, the slump ﬂow values were not changed as the replace- ment ratio of FBA was increased. Also, the slump ﬂow values of fresh concrete with 50% and 100% replacement of CBA were con- stant regardless of whether FBA was used. Notably, FBA absorbed a smaller amount of cement paste and water on the surface of the particle due to its lower porosity and water absorption, as shown in Table 2. Furthermore, the FBA absorbed only a very small amount of cement paste and water during mixing. This was due to the high viscosity of the cement paste applied in this study. That was, due to the high viscosity, little cement paste was absorbed in the tiny pores, which were around 0.1–10.0 lm i
